text: Sustainable development policy issues often arise in the interface between different policy domains or fields of politics. New technology might provide a trigger for creating new policy subsystems. Studying settings with many policy subsystems or domains therefore represents a major challenge for the research field. Building on sociological institutionalist research, we are working on a new theory for multi-system settings. First explorative research within this research line examines in more detail how belief coalitions form in a nascent policy subsystem for hydrogen in Germany. The new policy subsystem for hydrogen belongs to the interface of at least four sectors: the energy sector, the mobility sector, the heating and buildings sector and the industry sector. Also, the range of different stakeholders active within the policy subsystem adds to the complexity of the context. Analyzing a high level expert survey the research shows the presence of two belief coalitions. In one constellation a high number of stakeholders from the industry, NGOs and science are connected. In the expectable second coalition representatives from the gas and heating sector joined forces. Issues of production, application and imported hydrogen seem to divide the different actors in the policy subsystem. Yet most stakeholders agree on the suitability of hydrogen technology and strongly believe in the need of state action within the new policy subsystem. Explanations of why such coalition formations occurred can be inferred from a combination of approaches from the Advocacy Coalition Framework and from the politics of transitions literature. Several aspects contribute to belief coalition and policy subsystem formation such as - different sectoral characteristics of the involved actors, - characteristics of new technologies, - level of knowledge or lack thereof within a specific setting, as well as - prior trusting relationships between stakeholders. This paper represents the first results from an ambitious new approach in policy analysis dealing with complex multi-system settings.
